title: About Te Marautanga o Aotearoa and your mokopuna - Ministry of Education
content:
Mō Te Marautanga o Aotearoa me tō mokopuna

## About Te Marautanga o Aotearoa and your mokopuna
Find out about Te Marautanga o Aotearoa and how it supports your mokopuna to learn and achieve at kura.
Consultation on new Tau 11–13 subjects
The consultation for 26 subjects for Tau 11-13 is open until 15 June.
We are working with over 300 kaiako and tumuaki from kura and schools around the country to design and develop a new Tau 11–13 curriculum.
The first group of 26 subjects has been published. We welcome feedback from mokopuna , whānau and all of Aotearoa .
Phase 5 consultation – Tāhūrangi
The feedback will inform the final curriculum to be progressively implemented from 2028 to 2030.
In this video, find out how Te Marautanga o Aotearoa supports your mokopuna to thrive at kura.

### How the Te Marautanga o Aotearoa supports your mokopuna to thrive
Te Marautanga o Aotearoa – setting out what your mokopuna is expected to learn during each tau.
(Text on screen: Your child’s learning: How the curriculum supports your child to thrive.)
(The presenter is standing and talking, facing the camera.)
As parents and carers, we want the best for our children. And the New Zealand Curriculum and Te Marautanga o Aotearoa are designed to ensure just that.
(A class of primary school children writing at their desks.)
(The teacher talking to a classroom of kids.)
(A child hanging a note on a whiteboard.)
(Text on screen : Five phases/tūārere)
The strengthened national curriculum is organised into five phases, or tūārere of learning and includes year-by-year teaching and learning sequences.
(School children writing at their desks.)
This means your child | tamaiti will build on their last year's learning.
(Teachers hand pointing to lesson equipment.)
(School children writing at their desks.)
It sets out what your child | tamaiti is expected to learn over their time at school | kura including the intended levels of knowledge, understanding and skill to be achieved.
(Close up of child writing.)
The curricula are clear, sequenced and evidence-based.
(Teacher at a whiteboard.)
(Children writing at their desks.)
(Teacher using a large touch screen.)
(Teacher interacting with children.)
(Text on screen: Connecting current learning to existing knowledge.)
(Text on screen: Providing concise, step-by-step explanations.)
(Text on screen: Explaining, modelling and demonstrating new learning.)
(Text on screen: Checking understanding and providing feedback.)
(Text on screen: Providing opportunities to practice.)
It means teachers | kaiako will use explicit teaching which involves connecting current learning to what your child or tamaiti already knows, providing concise step-by-step explanations with your tamaiti or child's input, and discussion, explaining, modelling and demonstrating new learning, regularly checking for understanding and providing feedback and providing opportunities for your tamaiti or child to practice both in groups and independently.
(The presenter is standing and talking, facing the camera.)
The New Zealand Curriculum and Te Marautanga o Aotearoa are the building blocks that support your child's | tamaiti growth.
(Words on screen : Parent tip. To find out what your child will be learning during each year, check out ‘Understand your child’s learning’:
www.education.govt.nz/understand-learning )
Te Marautanga o Aotearoa sets out what your mokopuna is expected to learn during each tau at kura and what they should be achieving.
Te Marautanga o Aotearoa is for mokopuna learning in te reo Māori settings.
- Guide to Te Marautanga o Aotearoa – Tau 0 to 8
- Guide to Te Marautanga o Aotearoa – Tau 9 to 10
- Parents and caregivers
